pattern: A window's total of distinct values only changes when a value's in-window count crosses between zero and one, so tracking per-value counts alongside a running distinct-total lets a two-pointer window shrink and grow correctly even though duplicates don't add anything extra.
hints: The catch is that repeating a value inside the window doesn't add to the target sum a second time, so plain running-sum sliding window logic needs adjusting.

Keep a count per value that currently appears in the window and a separate running total of the distinct values present; when a value's count goes from 0 to 1 add it to the total, and when it drops back to 0 subtract it — that total is still monotonic enough in each pointer to support a standard grow-then-shrink window.

Expand the right pointer one step at a time, updating the count/total as above, then while the distinct total is at least the target, record the window length as a candidate answer and advance the left pointer (again updating count/total) until the window is no longer valid.

edge_inputs.description: sample: distinct pair sum reaches k with a length-2 window
sample: a single element already meets k
single-element array falling one short of k
many repeats of one huge value never accumulate past it alone; must combine with a second distinct value
k is unreachable even using every distinct value in the array
k is reachable only by taking the entire array as the window
all identical values: distinct sum never exceeds the single repeated value

stress.description: moderate-range random array with a k chosen to be reachable but not trivial, exercising the counted two-pointer logic at scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 400)
    return [nums, k]

stress.description: heavy-duplicate array (small value pool) crossed with a k near the max possible distinct sum, to stress the unreachable/-1 path and near-boundary windows
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size
    pool = [rng.randint(1, 9) for _ in range(9)]
    nums = [pool[rng.randint(0, len(pool) - 1)] for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 60)
    return [nums, k]
